checks.a_analysis: The HTML output correctly maintains the visual organization of the source presentation. Each slide is rendered as a distinct section in sequential order. Titles, bullet points, and media (images, icons, screenshots) are placed in a way that respects the original document's flow and intent. Multi-column slide layouts (like Slide 2 or Slide 7) are appropriately adapted to a single-column responsive flow without loss of meaning or clarity.

The document contains several complex tables, particularly on slides 29, 30, and 31. In the HTML output, these are rendered as functional HTML tables. They feature clear borders, distinct headers, and properly aligned cells. The content within the tables remains legible and the relational structure between rows and columns is preserved. No visual corruption or overlapping text is observed in the table regions.

A comprehensive review of the 60-page slide deck reveals a large number of meaningful images, including corporate logos (Slides 1, 60), headshots (Slide 2), illustrative icons (Slides 5, 6, 10, etc.), technical screenshots of software (Slides 8, 17, 19, etc.), and data visualizations (Slides 24, 36). All meaningful images from the source PDF are present and correctly rendered in the HTML output. No broken image links or missing content placeholders were found.

The HTML document was inspected for unintended content duplication. Beyond the expected repetition of headings in the Table of Contents and the document body, no genuine body content (such as entire paragraphs or tables) appears twice in the rendered output. Slide 18, which contains similar-looking list items, was confirmed to be a faithful rendering of the 'Before and After' examples present on that single source slide.
